quiz_questions.question_text: Regression-based predictions are used in:

options.text: Only statistics courses as a teaching exercise for students learning about data analysis
Weather, medicine, sports, business, and many other fields
Only physics experiments where precise mathematical relationships between variables are known
Only economics for forecasting market trends and gross domestic product estimates each quarter

quiz_questions.explanation: Regression is one of the most versatile predictive tools across disciplines.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cross-validation helps ensure:

options.text: The training set is large
The model generalizes well to new, unseen data
Overfitting
The test set is small

quiz_questions.explanation: Testing on held-out data checks real-world predictive performance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A prediction interval is __ than a confidence interval for the mean response:

options.text: Wider
Always zero-width
The same width
Narrower

quiz_questions.explanation: Prediction intervals include both model uncertainty and individual observation variability.

quiz_questions.question_text: Training data is used to:

options.text: Build (fit) the model
Test the model
Replace the model
Validate predictions

quiz_questions.explanation: Training data is used to estimate model parameters.

quiz_questions.question_text: Testing data is used to:

options.text: Build the model by fitting coefficients to the observed data points in the training set
Increase sample size by generating additional synthetic data points from the training set
Evaluate how well the model predicts unseen observations
Replace outliers with predicted values to clean up the dataset before final model fitting

quiz_questions.explanation: Testing on new data checks the model's generalization ability.

quiz_questions.question_text: Overfitting a prediction model means it:

options.text: Fits training data too closely and performs poorly on new data
Has low r-squared, indicating the model fails to capture meaningful variation in the data
Predicts well on new data because it learned the true underlying pattern in the training set
Is too simple to capture any useful patterns or relationships present in the training data

quiz_questions.explanation: Overfitting captures noise, reducing the model's ability to generalize.

quiz_questions.question_text: K-fold cross-validation works by:

options.text: Never validating the model, instead using all data for training to maximize the model's accuracy
Using all data for training only, without setting aside any observations for validation purposes
Splitting data into k parts, training on k−1 and testing on the remaining fold — repeated k times
Only using one observation for testing while the rest are exclusively used for building the model

quiz_questions.explanation: Each fold gets a turn as the test set, producing k estimates of model performance.

quiz_questions.question_text: Prediction models should be updated when:

options.text: Never, because once a model is validated it remains accurate indefinitely
New data becomes available or the underlying process changes
Only at year-end when annual performance reviews of models are conducted on schedule
Only when r-squared drops below 0.5, indicating the model's explanatory power has weakened

quiz_questions.explanation: Models must evolve with changing data and real-world conditions.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Moneyball approach used:

options.text: Only batting average to evaluate players because it was the most traditional offensive statistic
Regression and analytics to identify undervalued players
Random selection of players from a pool of available free agents without any data analysis at all
No statistics at all, relying entirely on the scouts' subjective evaluations of player potential

quiz_questions.explanation: The Oakland A's used statistical models to find players whose value was overlooked by traditional scouting.

quiz_questions.question_text: The best prediction models balance:

options.text: Complexity and generalizability (parsimony)
Maximum complexity and minimum data
Only simplicity
Only training accuracy

quiz_questions.explanation: Models should be complex enough to capture real patterns but simple enough to generalize.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a prediction interval?

options.text: An interval that estimates the range in which a single new observation is likely to fall, accounting for both model uncertainty and individual variability
An interval that estimates the mean of y for all observations at a given x value in the population
The width of the residual plot measured from its lowest to its highest vertical point in the chart
The range of x values in the dataset over which the regression model can make valid predictions

quiz_questions.explanation: Prediction intervals are wider than confidence intervals because they must cover not just where the average falls but where an individual observation might land.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'cross-validation' mean?

options.text: Crossing out variables from the model
Checking the model on the same data it was built on
A technique that repeatedly splits data into training and testing sets to evaluate how well a model generalizes to new data
Validating only the intercept of the model

quiz_questions.explanation: Cross-validation tests predictive performance on data the model has not seen during training.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is feature engineering?

options.text: Removing all variables from a model to reduce complexity and improve interpretability of results
Using only the original raw variables in their unmodified form without applying any transformations
A mechanical engineering technique for designing physical features and structural components
Creating new variables from existing ones (e.g., interactions, ratios, transformations) to improve model performance

quiz_questions.explanation: Feature engineering creates derived variables that may capture patterns the original variables alone do not.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between a confidence interval for the mean response and a prediction interval?

options.text: A prediction interval is always narrower because it estimates a single specific y value at a given x
A confidence interval estimates the average y at a given x; a prediction interval estimates where a single new y will fall — the prediction interval is always wider
They are the same thing and can be used interchangeably depending on the analysis context at hand
A confidence interval is for x values and a prediction interval is for y values in the regression model

quiz_questions.explanation: The prediction interval is wider because it adds individual variation on top of the uncertainty about the mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the training-testing split in predictive modeling?

options.text: Testing only the intercept
Using all data to both build and evaluate the model
Training the data to follow a specific pattern
Dividing data into a training set (to build the model) and a testing set (to evaluate its predictions on unseen data)

quiz_questions.explanation: Holding out a test set prevents overfitting and gives a realistic measure of how the model performs on new data.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'generalizability' mean for a prediction model?

options.text: The model has R² = 1
The model uses as many variables as possible
The model can perfectly predict every training observation
The model's predictions are accurate not just on the data it was trained on, but also on new, unseen data

quiz_questions.explanation: A generalizable model captures the true underlying pattern rather than sample-specific noise.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the Framingham Heart Study risk score illustrate about prediction models?

options.text: Medical models do not use statistics because patient outcomes are too unpredictable and variable
Prediction models are only useful in finance because medical data does not follow regression patterns
Only one variable is needed to predict health outcomes because diseases have single root causes
Regression-based models can use multiple patient factors (age, blood pressure, cholesterol) to predict health outcomes like cardiovascular disease risk

quiz_questions.explanation: The Framingham score is a classic example of multiple regression applied to medicine, combining several risk factors into a single prediction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between machine learning regression and classical regression?

options.text: Machine learning emphasizes prediction accuracy and cross-validation, while classical regression also emphasizes interpretation and inference about parameters
Classical regression cannot make predictions and is used only for describing existing relationships
Machine learning does not use data and instead relies on theoretical assumptions about the system
They use completely different mathematics with no overlap in their underlying statistical frameworks

quiz_questions.explanation: Both share mathematical foundations, but ML focuses on prediction while classical statistics also prioritizes understanding relationships.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'model updating' refer to?

options.text: Revising a prediction model when new data becomes available or when the underlying conditions change, to maintain accuracy
Replacing the model with a completely different one every day
Never changing a model once it is built
Updating only the intercept

quiz_questions.explanation: Real-world conditions evolve, so models must be re-evaluated and updated to remain relevant.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'customer churn prediction'?

options.text: Churning butter using statistical methods to optimize the manufacturing production process
Predicting stock prices based on historical trading patterns and market fundamental indicators
Using regression and other models to identify customers who are likely to stop using a service, allowing companies to intervene
Predicting which products will sell the most based on seasonal demand and inventory analysis

quiz_questions.explanation: Churn prediction helps businesses retain customers by identifying at-risk individuals before they leave.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hospital builds a regression model to predict patient readmission within 30 days using age, diagnosis severity, and length of stay. The model has r² = 0.42 on the training data and r² = 0.38 on the test data. What does the small difference in r² values suggest?

options.text: The model is overfitting badly because the training r-squared is slightly higher than the testing
The model is useless because r-squared is below 0.5 on the test data and cannot predict accurately
The test set was too large, reducing the training data available and weakening the model's accuracy
The model generalizes reasonably well — the small gap between training and testing r² indicates minimal overfitting

quiz_questions.explanation: A small drop from training to testing r² indicates the model captures real patterns rather than noise, even though moderate r² is common in healthcare.

quiz_questions.question_text: A weather service uses a regression model with 12 variables (temperature, humidity, wind speed, etc.) to predict rainfall. After 5-fold cross-validation, the average prediction error is 3.2 mm. If the model were only tested on its training data, the error would be 1.8 mm. Why is the cross-validation error higher?

options.text: Cross-validation always produces larger r-squared numbers by design due to the averaging process
The weather changed between folds, so each fold experienced fundamentally different climate patterns
The model performs better on data it was trained on; cross-validation gives a more honest estimate of true prediction error on new data
Cross-validation uses fewer variables, which automatically reduces the model's explanatory power

quiz_questions.explanation: Training error is optimistically low because the model has already seen those data; cross-validation provides a realistic estimate of out-of-sample performance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sports analytics team predicts player performance using age, games played, and training hours. For a 25-year-old with 80 games and 500 training hours, the predicted score is 78 with a 95% prediction interval of (62, 94) and a 95% confidence interval for the mean of (74, 82). A scout asks 'Will this specific player score at least 70?' Which interval should be used?

options.text: Neither interval is appropriate
The point estimate of 78 is all that is needed
The prediction interval (62, 94), because it accounts for individual variability — the question is about one specific player
The confidence interval (74, 82), because it is more precise

quiz_questions.explanation: For predicting an individual outcome, the prediction interval is appropriate because it captures individual variation beyond just the mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: A retail company builds a sales forecast model in 2020. By 2023, online shopping has grown significantly and consumer behavior has shifted. The model's predictions are increasingly inaccurate. What should the company do?

options.text: Abandon statistical modeling entirely
Double the number of predictors without new data
Update the model with recent data that reflects current shopping behavior and market conditions
Continue using the 2020 model because it was accurate then

quiz_questions.explanation: When the underlying process changes (structural change), models must be refitted with recent data to remain accurate.

quiz_questions.question_text: An insurance company uses regression to predict annual claim costs from driver age, vehicle type, and driving record. They test three models: Model A (2 variables, r² = 0.45 on test), Model B (5 variables, r² = 0.52 on test), Model C (20 variables, r² = 0.48 on test). Which model is likely best?

options.text: Model B — it has the highest test r², suggesting it captures real patterns without overfitting
Model A — fewer variables is always best
Model C — more variables always produce better predictions
None of them because r² should be above 0.8

quiz_questions.explanation: Model C's lower test r² compared to B suggests overfitting from too many variables. Model B balances complexity and predictive power.

quiz_questions.question_text: A city uses regression to predict daily subway ridership from day of week, weather, and whether there is a special event. On a rainy Tuesday with a concert downtown, the model predicts 450,000 riders. The actual count was 485,000. The residual is:

options.text: +35,000 (the actual ridership exceeded the prediction)
−35,000 (the model overpredicted)
0 (the model is perfect)

quiz_questions.explanation: Residual = actual − predicted = 485,000 − 450,000 = +35,000. The positive residual means the model underpredicted.

quiz_questions.question_text: A bank uses a credit scoring model to predict loan default probability. The model was built on data from 2015-2019. During the 2020 pandemic, default rates changed dramatically and the model performed poorly. This illustrates:

options.text: Credit scoring is not a valid use of regression because human behavior cannot be modeled statistically
Regression models are always unreliable when applied to financial data with high natural variability
The 2015-2019 data was collected incorrectly, so the model was flawed from the very beginning of development
Models built on historical data may fail when unprecedented events change the underlying relationships, highlighting the need for model monitoring and updating

quiz_questions.explanation: Structural breaks — like a pandemic — violate the assumption that future data follows the same pattern as training data.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marketing team performs 10-fold cross-validation on a customer spending model. The model has r² = 0.65 on training and r² = 0.62 on average across the 10 test folds. The manager asks, 'Can we use this model for next quarter's budget planning?'

options.text: No, the model is useless because r-squared is not above 0.8 on either training or testing data
Only if r-squared equals 1 on training data, indicating the model captures all variation perfectly
No, cross-validation shows the model cannot be used in practice since the r-squared varies by fold
Yes — the consistent performance across folds (small gap) suggests the model is reliable for planning, though predictions will have some uncertainty

quiz_questions.explanation: Stable cross-validation results indicate reliable generalization; the model can inform planning while acknowledging the inherent uncertainty.

quiz_questions.question_text: A health clinic builds a model predicting blood pressure from BMI, age, and smoking status. An engineer suggests also adding shoe size and favorite color as predictors. What is likely to happen?

options.text: Adding more variables always improves a model regardless of whether they are relevant to the outcome
R-squared will increase substantially and predictions will improve once the new variables are included
The model will reject the variables automatically through its built-in variable selection mechanism
R² may increase slightly on training data, but the irrelevant variables will likely decrease adjusted R² and worsen predictions on new data (overfitting)

quiz_questions.explanation: Adding irrelevant predictors inflates R² through noise but hurts adjusted R² and test-set performance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A university admissions office uses a regression model to predict first-year GPA from SAT score, high school GPA, and extracurricular hours. They notice that after a new feature — parent income — is added, prediction improves significantly. However, using income raises ethical concerns about fairness. What should they consider?

options.text: Ethics have no place in statistical modeling because accuracy is the only criterion that matters
Prediction accuracy must be weighed against ethical considerations, equity, and potential bias — a more accurate model is not always a better model if it discriminates unfairly
Always use every variable that improves prediction, regardless of ethical concerns or potential bias
Remove all predictors to avoid bias entirely, since any variable could introduce unfair discrimination

quiz_questions.explanation: Responsible prediction modeling considers both accuracy and fairness; including sensitive variables may perpetuate systemic inequalities.
